annotations.label: Trigonometric ratios

anchor.snippet: Trigonometric ratios are the ratios between edges of a right triangle

annotations.note: Mathlib defines sin/cos/tan analytically and separately proves their right-triangle edge-ratio characterizations (e.g. sin·hypotenuse = opposite), rather than defining the ratios directly.

annotations.label: Hypotenuse, adjacent, and opposite sides

anchor.snippet: the hypotenuse is the side opposite to the 90-degree angle in a right triangle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named definitions for hypotenuse/adjacent/opposite; right-triangle results just use dist between points with a π/2 angle.

annotations.label: Sine

anchor.snippet: Sine (denoted sin), defined as the ratio of the side opposite the angle to the hypotenuse.

annotations.note: Real.sin is the sine function (defined analytically for all reals, generalizing the right-triangle ratio confirmed by sin_angle_mul_dist_of_angle_eq_pi_div_two).

annotations.label: Cosine

anchor.snippet: Cosine (denoted cos), defined as the ratio of the adjacent leg

annotations.note: Real.cos is the cosine function defined analytically for all reals, with the adjacent/hypotenuse ratio proven via cos_angle_mul_dist_of_angle_eq_pi_div_two.

annotations.label: Tangent

anchor.snippet: Tangent (denoted tan), defined as the ratio of the opposite leg to the adjacent leg.

annotations.note: Real.tan is defined as sin/cos, generalizing the opposite/adjacent ratio proven via tan_angle_mul_dist_of_angle_eq_pi_div_two.

annotations.label: Cosecant, secant, cotangent

anchor.snippet: The reciprocals of these ratios are named the cosecant (csc), secant (sec), and cotangent (cot)

annotations.note: Cotangent (Real.cot/Complex.cot) is defined, but cosecant and secant have no dedicated definitions in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cofunction relationship with complementary angle

anchor.snippet: The cosine, cotangent, and cosecant are so named because they are respectively the sine, tangent, and secant of the complementary angle

annotations.note: cos(π/2−x)=sin x is Real.cos_pi_div_two_sub, with companions Real.sin_pi_div_two_sub and Real.tan_pi_div_two_sub.

annotations.label: Unit circle

anchor.section: The unit circle and common trigonometric values
anchor.snippet: the unit circle , which is the circle of radius 1 centered at the origin in the plane

annotations.note: Circle is defined as the metric sphere of radius 1 centered at 0 in ℂ (identified with the plane).

annotations.label: Inverse trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: By restricting the domain of a trigonometric function, however, they can be made invertible

annotations.note: Real.arcsin, Real.arccos (in Trigonometric.Inverse) and Real.arctan are defined as domain-restricted inverses of the trig functions.

annotations.label: Maclaurin series for sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: the trigonometric ratios can be represented by Maclaurin series

annotations.note: Complex.hasSum_sin/Complex.hasSum_cos (and Real.hasSum_sin/Real.hasSum_cos) give the standard power-series expansions of sine and cosine.

annotations.label: Complex exponential formula

anchor.snippet: the following formula holds for the complex exponential

annotations.note: Complex.exp_mul_I states exp(x·I) = cos x + sin x·I, exactly the e^{ix}=cos x + i sin x identity.

annotations.label: Other historical trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: there are additional trigonometric functions that were historically important

annotations.note: Historical functions such as versine, haversine, exsecant, and chord are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fourier series of periodic functions

anchor.snippet: Fourier discovered that every continuous , periodic function could be described as an infinite sum of trigonometric functions

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes L²-convergence of Fourier series on AddCircle (hasSum_fourier_series_L2, fourierBasis), not the general continuous-function pointwise statement.

annotations.label: Law of sines

anchor.snippet: The law of sines (also known as the "sine rule") for an arbitrary triangle states

annotations.note: The law of sines is proven as sin_angle_mul_dist_eq_sin_angle_mul_dist (aliased law_sin).

annotations.label: Law of cosines

anchor.snippet: The law of cosines (known as the cosine formula, or the "cos rule") is an extension of the Pythagorean theorem to arbitrary triangles

annotations.note: The law of cosines is proven as dist_sq_eq_…_cos_angle (aliased law_cos).

annotations.label: Law of tangents

anchor.snippet: The law of tangents , developed by François Viète , is an alternative to the law of cosines

annotations.note: No law-of-tangents statement was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Area of a triangle

anchor.snippet: the area of the triangle is given by half the product of the lengths of two sides and the sine of the angle between the two sides

annotations.note: Mathlib has signed-area-form infrastructure (areaForm, abs_areaForm_le/_of_orthogonal) but no explicit theorem giving triangle area as ½·a·b·sin C.

annotations.label: Pythagorean identities

anchor.snippet: The following trigonometric identities are related to the Pythagorean theorem and hold for any value

annotations.note: Real.sin_sq_add_cos_sq gives sin²+cos²=1, with the tangent/secant forms via Real.one_add_tan_sq_mul_cos_sq_eq_one.

annotations.label: Euler's formula

anchor.snippet: produces the following analytical identities for sine, cosine, and tangent in terms of e and the imaginary unit i

annotations.note: Complex.sin and Complex.cos are defined exactly by these exp-based identities ((exp(-zI)−exp(zI))·I/2 and (exp(zI)+exp(-zI))/2), with Complex.tan their quotient.
